explanation.title: Making Decisions with if, else if, and else

codeExample.title: Exam Grade Classifier
codeExample.explanation:
- `let grade, feedback;` — declares two `let` variables without assigning them yet; they will be set inside the branches
- `if (score >= 90)` — the parentheses hold the boolean condition; `>=` is greater-than-or-equal
- `} else if (score >= 80) {` — only evaluated when `score < 90`; once a branch matches, the rest are skipped
- `grade = "B";` — variables declared with `let` outside the block are still in scope inside it
- `if (promoCode)` — a non-empty string is truthy; if `promoCode` were `""` or `null`, the block would not run

exercise.title: Write an Age Category Classifier
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that classifies a person into an age category. Declare const age = 17. Use if/else if/else to assign a category string: under 13 is 'Child', 13 to 17 is 'Teenager', 18 to 59 is 'Adult', and 60 or above is 'Senior'. Print the age and the category on separate labelled lines.

Expected output with age = 17:
Age: 17
Category: Teenager
hints: 💡 Add else if (age <= 59) { category = "Adult"; } after the Teenager branch.
💡 The final category (Senior) needs only else { category = "Senior"; } because all lower ages are already handled.
💡 Each else if only needs to check the upper bound — the lower bound is guaranteed by the previous branch having been false.

questions.question: How many else if branches can a single if statement have in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: There is no limit on the number of else if branches. You can chain as many as your logic requires. JavaScript evaluates them top to bottom and executes the first branch whose condition is true. If none match, the optional else block runs.

questions.question: What does the grade classifier print when score = 85, given: if (score >= 90) A; else if (score >= 80) B; else C?
questions.explanation: JavaScript evaluates conditions top to bottom and stops at the first match. score >= 90 is false (85 < 90), so it moves to else if (score >= 80). Since 85 >= 80 is true, it prints B and skips the else. Only one branch executes in an if/else if/else chain.
